name: Zulu
description: The Zulu are the largest South African ethnic group, concentrated in KwaZulu-Natal. Under King Shaka (c.1787–1828), the Zulu Kingdom rose from a small clan to a military superpower that dominated southern Africa through the Mfecane (the 'crushing' — a period of widespread warfare and migration). Shaka's military innovations — the short stabbing spear (assegai), the chest-and-horns battle formation, and rigorous warrior training — produced an army that defeated British imperial forces at the Battle of Isandlwana (1879).
cultural_highlights: Shaka's military revolution — the bull-horn battle formation, the stabbing assegai (iklwa), and the impi (regiment) system transformed southern African warfare
Reed Dance (Umkhosi Womhlanga) — annual ceremony where thousands of young women present reeds to the king, celebrating female purity and royal prestige
Beadwork language — Zulu beadwork uses colour combinations to send specific messages about love, status, and relationship; a complete non-verbal communication system
Ubuntu philosophy — 'umuntu ngumuntu ngabantu' ('a person is a person through other persons'); a foundational social ethics of interconnectedness
Isicathamiya music — a cappella close-harmony singing popularised globally by Ladysmith Black Mambazo

food_traditions.name: Umngqusho (samp and beans)
food_traditions.description: Crushed maize kernels cooked with sugar beans — a slow-cooked staple eaten daily across South Africa; Nelson Mandela's favourite dish

food_traditions.name: Amasi (fermented milk)
food_traditions.description: Naturally fermented milk with a thick yogurt-like consistency — the everyday Zulu beverage and accompaniment to cooked maize

food_traditions.name: Uphuthu (crumbled maize)
food_traditions.description: Crumbled dry ugali-like maize eaten with amasi — a lighter alternative to solid ugali

food_traditions.name: Braai (grilled meat)
food_traditions.description: Communal grilling of beef, lamb, and wors (boerewors sausage) — a shared South African tradition with Zulu ceremonial roots

music_traditions: Isicathamiya — close-harmony a cappella singing performed in midnight competitions; made global by Ladysmith Black Mambazo
Ingoma (warrior dance songs) — sung by male groups in unison with foot-stamping; recreating military ceremonies
Maskanda — traditional Zulu guitar and concertina music blending Western instruments with Zulu compositional techniques
